intro:
Clauda (also spelled Cauda) was a small island situated approximately twenty-three miles southwest of Cape Matala on the southern coast of Crete. It appears in the New Testament in the account of Paul's voyage to Rome, when the ship carrying him was caught by a violent northeastern wind called the Euraquilo (Acts 27:16). Under the lee of Clauda the crew gained a brief respite that allowed them to secure the ship's boat, undergird the hull with cables, and prepare for the storm ahead.

The island offered only temporary shelter before the ship was driven westward toward the sandbanks of Syrtis off the North African coast. Clauda's significance in the narrative lies entirely in this single episode, yet the nautical precision of Acts 27 — noting the difficulty of managing the ship's boat even under the island's partial shelter — lends strong historical credibility to the eyewitness account. The island is identified with modern Gavdos, the southernmost point of Europe, which lies in roughly the position described by the Acts narrative.
